turns.content: I'm getting this panic: 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: InvalidConfig'. I tried to unwrap the result of my config parser but it seems like the error isn't propagating correctly. What went wrong with how I structured my return type?

The issue is likely that you're returning a specific error type directly instead of wrapping the source error in your custom type. When you unwrap a Result, it panics immediately if the inner value is an Err. You should use the Error trait to chain errors so that when someone calls .source() on your custom error, they get the original parser error. This way the context travels with the data without losing information.

Ah okay, so I should be using std::error::Error and maybe calling something like .context()? But my custom type doesn't seem to implement the trait methods correctly. How do I actually build this chain?

You need to implement the Error trait on your custom struct. Inside that implementation, you'll call .new() to create an instance and then use .set_source() or .context() to attach the underlying error. For downcasting when you need to inspect the inner value later, you should also implement std::any::Any so you can safely cast from Any back to your specific type using any_downcast_ref(). This lets you handle different error scenarios in one generic function.

That makes sense for the chain, but I'm still struggling with downcasting. When I try to use any_cast() on my wrapped error it seems to fail or return None unexpectedly. Is there a specific pattern I should follow when combining generic functions with downcasting?

The key is to always start by calling .downcast_ref::<Any>() on your Error trait implementation rather than trying to cast directly. This returns an Option<&dyn Any>. If you then call .downcast_ref::<YourCustomError>() on that option, it will safely unwrap only if the type matches. For generic error printing functions, you can match on the dyn Any interface or use a helper function that takes &dyn Error and delegates to specific implementations based on runtime type checks.

I'm trying to build a generic error printer for my CLI tool, but I keep hitting dead ends when trying to access the underlying cause. My code looks like this: let err = Box::new(MyError::from(std::io::Error::last_os_error())); println!("{}", std::error::Error::source(err));

Hey, that's a common stumbling block. Think of the Error trait like a nested box where each layer hides part of the problem; your source() call is trying to peek inside the inner box but needs the right key to unlock it. The real trick here is using Any to downcast without knowing the exact type ahead of time, so you can safely unwrap the chain regardless of what specific error wrapped it.
Ah, I see. So if I have a Box<dyn Error>, how do I actually get to the source? I tried using as_any() but got a compile error saying I can't call methods on an object without a trait.

Right, that's exactly why we need to downcast first. You should use Any::down_ref() or Any::downcast_mut() depending on whether you want immutable access or a mutable reference to the inner error type. This lets you safely cast the dyn Error into its concrete type so you can call methods like source() that aren't visible through the dynamic slice.
Got it, I managed to downcast successfully and now my function looks clean. But I'm still getting some weird warnings about orphan traits when trying to use std::io::Error in a generic context.

Those warnings usually show up because you're mixing non-generic types with generics without care. The solution is to define your helper function so it takes &dyn Error and then uses the downcast pattern internally, letting the compiler infer the concrete type from your actual error instance rather than forcing a static assumption about what type might be inside.

I'm working on a new crate for our startup and I pasted this error: 'Cannot cast to Error'. It says I can't use `?` with my custom type. I thought I just needed to implement the trait like I did with serde_json::Value, so why isn't it working?

Hey! First off, don't panic—that's a common trip-up for juniors when they start touching std::error::Error. The reason you can't just `?` your error is that the trait requires more than just a single method; it needs a source() chain and downcasting via Any so we can build generic error-printing functions later without breaking everything. You need to implement both Source and Error fully, not just one.
Oh, I see. So I should add a method that returns Option<Self> for downcasting? And maybe use the Result type somewhere?

You're getting closer but there's one detail missing: you need to implement source() correctly so it either returns Some(other error) or None. If we leave that out, our Error trait won't work with the standard library's error handling systems. Also, remember you can use Result<T, E> as a wrapper around your custom type if needed for specific cases in your crate.
